title: How Honeybees Communicate
passage:
Honeybees have one of the most sophisticated systems of communication known among insects. When a forager bee discovers a rich source of nectar or pollen, she returns to the hive and performs a series of movements known as the waggle dance. During this dance, the bee moves in a figure-eight pattern, running straight through the middle of the loop while rapidly shaking her abdomen from side to side. The direction of this straight run, measured relative to the vertical, encodes the direction of the food source relative to the sun. The duration of the waggle portion communicates the distance: a longer waggle indicates that the food lies farther away.

Remarkably, other bees crowd around the dancer in the darkness of the hive, following her movements closely and even touching her with their antennae. Because the interior of the hive is dark, the audience cannot see the dance in the usual sense; instead, they perceive the vibrations and the airflow the dancer produces. After decoding this information, the recruited bees fly out and locate the food, often with impressive accuracy over distances of several kilometers.

The biologist Karl von Frisch first decoded the waggle dance in the mid-twentieth century, an achievement that earned him a Nobel Prize. His conclusion that a simple insect could convey abstract spatial information was initially met with skepticism, since many scientists doubted that such symbolic communication existed outside of humans. Later experiments, however, confirmed that bees genuinely use the dance to guide one another, though they also rely on scent cues to pinpoint the exact location once they arrive nearby.

questions.q: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Honeybees rely mainly on scent rather than movement to find food.
Honeybees use a dance to communicate the location of food sources.
Karl von Frisch was the first scientist to study insect behavior.
The interior of a beehive is too dark for bees to see one another.
questions.explanation: The passage centers on the waggle dance and how it conveys the direction and distance of food. The other choices describe supporting details or are contradicted by the text.

questions.q: According to the passage, what does the duration of the waggle run indicate?
options: How rich the food source is
The direction of the food relative to the sun
How far away the food source is
The number of bees needed to collect the food
questions.explanation: The passage states that a longer waggle indicates that the food lies farther away, so duration encodes distance. Direction is encoded by the angle of the run, not its duration.

questions.q: How do bees inside the hive perceive the dance if they cannot see it?
options: They smell the nectar carried by the dancer.
They sense the vibrations and airflow the dancer produces.
They follow a trail of pollen left on the honeycomb.
They listen for a high-pitched buzzing sound.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that because the hive is dark, the audience perceives the vibrations and the airflow the dancer produces rather than seeing the dance.

questions.q: Why was von Frisch's conclusion initially met with skepticism?
options: Many scientists doubted that symbolic communication existed outside of humans.
His experiments could not be repeated by other researchers.
Bees were thought to be unable to fly long distances.
The Nobel committee questioned the accuracy of his data.
questions.explanation: The passage says skepticism arose because many scientists doubted that such symbolic communication existed outside of humans.

title: The Life Cycle of Stars
passage:
Stars are not permanent fixtures of the sky; they are born, they age, and eventually they die. A star begins its life inside a giant cloud of gas and dust called a nebula. Over time, gravity causes regions of the cloud to collapse inward. As the material contracts, it heats up, and when the central temperature becomes high enough, nuclear fusion ignites. Fusion converts hydrogen into helium and releases enormous amounts of energy, which is what makes the star shine.

For most of its life, a star exists in a stable phase called the main sequence, during which the outward pressure from fusion balances the inward pull of gravity. Our own Sun is currently a middle-aged main-sequence star. How long a star remains on the main sequence depends chiefly on its mass. Counterintuitively, more massive stars burn through their fuel far more quickly than smaller ones and therefore have much shorter lives, sometimes lasting only a few million years rather than billions.

When a star exhausts the hydrogen in its core, its structure changes dramatically. The core contracts while the outer layers expand and cool, turning the star into a red giant. What happens next depends again on mass. A star like the Sun will shed its outer layers and leave behind a dense, slowly cooling remnant called a white dwarf. Much more massive stars end their lives in a catastrophic explosion known as a supernova, which can briefly outshine an entire galaxy and scatter heavy elements into space. These scattered elements later become the raw material for new stars and planets.

questions.q: What causes a star to begin forming within a nebula?
options: A nearby supernova pushes the gas together.
Gravity causes regions of the cloud to collapse inward.
Nuclear fusion begins spontaneously in the cold gas.
The cloud cools until it turns into solid dust.
questions.explanation: The passage states that gravity causes regions of the cloud to collapse inward, which begins the star-forming process. Fusion only ignites later, after the material has heated up.

questions.q: According to the passage, why do more massive stars have shorter lives?
options: They are located farther from other stars.
They contain less hydrogen to begin with.
They burn through their fuel far more quickly.
They never reach the main sequence stage.
questions.explanation: The passage explicitly says more massive stars burn through their fuel far more quickly and therefore have much shorter lives.

questions.q: What does the passage say happens to a star like the Sun after the red giant stage?
options: It explodes as a supernova.
It collapses directly into a nebula.
It sheds its outer layers and leaves a white dwarf.
It returns to the main sequence.
questions.explanation: The passage states that a star like the Sun will shed its outer layers and leave behind a white dwarf, while only much more massive stars become supernovae.

questions.q: Why are supernovae described as important beyond the death of a single star?
options: They scatter heavy elements that become material for new stars and planets.
They pull nearby stars into the main sequence.
They create nebulae that block starlight permanently.
They cause neighboring stars to collapse into white dwarfs.
questions.explanation: The passage notes that supernovae scatter heavy elements into space, and these later become the raw material for new stars and planets.

title: The Printing Press and the Spread of Ideas
passage:
Before the middle of the fifteenth century, books in Europe were copied by hand, usually by monks working slowly in monasteries. Producing a single copy of a lengthy text could take months, and as a result books were rare and expensive. Only the wealthy, the church, and a few institutions could afford large collections. This scarcity limited literacy and slowed the exchange of new ideas.

The situation changed dramatically after Johannes Gutenberg introduced a printing press with movable metal type around 1450. Movable type allowed individual letters to be arranged, inked, and pressed onto paper, then rearranged to print a different page. Once a page was set, hundreds of identical copies could be produced quickly. The cost of books fell sharply, and their numbers multiplied. Within a few decades, printing presses had appeared in cities across Europe.

The consequences reached far beyond the book trade. As printed materials became affordable, literacy spread among ordinary people, and information could travel faster and more widely than ever before. Religious reformers, scientists, and political thinkers used the press to circulate their arguments to large audiences, sometimes in defiance of established authorities. Historians often point to the printing press as a key factor in the Protestant Reformation and the Scientific Revolution, because it enabled controversial ideas to spread before they could be suppressed. In this way, a change in technology helped reshape the intellectual and social landscape of an entire continent.

questions.q: Why were books rare and expensive before the mid-fifteenth century?
options: Paper had not yet been invented in Europe.
They were copied slowly by hand.
Governments strictly limited who could own them.
Few people knew how to read them.
questions.explanation: The passage states that books were copied by hand and that producing a single copy could take months, which made them rare and expensive.

questions.q: What key feature of Gutenberg's press is emphasized in the passage?
options: It printed images as well as text.
It used water power to run automatically.
Its movable type could be rearranged for different pages.
It required no ink to make copies.
questions.explanation: The passage highlights movable metal type, which allowed letters to be arranged, pressed, and then rearranged to print a different page.

questions.q: According to the passage, what was one social effect of cheaper printed materials?
options: Literacy spread among ordinary people.
Monasteries grew wealthier from copying books.
Fewer people wanted to read new ideas.
Governments gained tighter control over information.
questions.explanation: The passage says that as printed materials became affordable, literacy spread among ordinary people and information traveled faster and wider.

questions.q: Why do historians connect the printing press to movements like the Reformation?
options: It allowed the church to censor opposing views quickly.
It enabled controversial ideas to spread before they could be suppressed.
It reduced the number of scientists in Europe.
It made handwritten books more valuable.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that the press enabled controversial ideas to spread widely before they could be suppressed, which is why it is linked to the Reformation and Scientific Revolution.

title: Supply, Demand, and Price
passage:
In a market economy, the prices of most goods are not set by any single authority. Instead, they emerge from the interaction of two forces: supply and demand. Demand refers to how much of a good buyers are willing and able to purchase at various prices. In general, when the price of a good rises, buyers tend to purchase less of it, and when the price falls, they purchase more. Supply refers to how much of a good producers are willing to offer for sale at various prices. Producers usually offer more of a good when its price is high, because higher prices make production more profitable.

The price at which the quantity buyers want to purchase exactly equals the quantity producers want to sell is called the equilibrium price. At this price, the market clears: there is neither a surplus of unsold goods nor a shortage of buyers left unsatisfied. If the price happens to be set above equilibrium, producers will offer more than buyers wish to purchase, creating a surplus that pushes prices back down. If the price is below equilibrium, eager buyers will compete for scarce goods, driving prices up.

These adjustments help explain why prices change over time. Suppose a poor harvest reduces the supply of a crop. With less available, the good becomes scarcer, and its price tends to rise until the quantity demanded falls to match the smaller supply. Conversely, if a new technology allows producers to make a good more cheaply, supply increases and prices tend to fall. Understanding these basic relationships allows economists to predict, at least roughly, how markets will respond to changing conditions.

questions.q: What is the passage mainly about?
options: How governments set the prices of goods
How supply and demand determine prices in a market
Why poor harvests always cause economic crises
How new technologies are invented
questions.explanation: The passage explains how the interaction of supply and demand determines prices, making that its central idea.

questions.q: According to the passage, what typically happens to demand when a price rises?
options: Buyers purchase more of the good.
Buyers purchase less of the good.
Producers offer less of the good.
The equilibrium price disappears.
questions.explanation: The passage states that when the price of a good rises, buyers tend to purchase less of it.

questions.q: What is the equilibrium price?
options: The highest price producers can legally charge
The price at which quantity demanded equals quantity supplied
The average of all prices over a year
The price set by the largest producer in a market
questions.explanation: The passage defines the equilibrium price as the price at which the quantity buyers want to purchase exactly equals the quantity producers want to sell.

questions.q: What does the passage predict will happen if a poor harvest reduces supply?
options: Prices tend to rise until demand matches the smaller supply.
Prices stay the same because demand is fixed.
Producers create a large surplus of the crop.
The equilibrium price falls sharply.
questions.explanation: The passage says that when supply falls, the good becomes scarcer and its price tends to rise until quantity demanded falls to match the smaller supply.

title: The Invention of Perspective in Painting
passage:
For much of the Middle Ages, European painters did not attempt to depict space in a realistic way. Figures were often arranged flatly on the surface, with important people drawn larger than others regardless of where they stood. Distant objects were not systematically made smaller, and there was little sense of a coherent three-dimensional world behind the picture's surface. Paintings were meant to convey spiritual meaning rather than to imitate the appearance of the visible world.

During the early fifteenth century in Italy, artists and architects developed a technique known as linear perspective that transformed the way space was represented. Linear perspective is based on the observation that parallel lines appear to converge as they recede toward a single vanishing point on the horizon, and that objects appear smaller the farther away they are. By following precise geometric rules, a painter could create the convincing illusion of depth on a flat panel or wall, so that a viewer seemed to look through the surface into a real space beyond.

The architect Filippo Brunelleschi is usually credited with demonstrating the principles of linear perspective, and the painter Masaccio was among the first to apply them in major works. The technique spread rapidly among Renaissance artists, who prized it as a mark of skill and sophistication. Perspective did more than improve realism; it reflected a broader cultural interest in mathematics, observation, and the rational ordering of the world. In this sense, a development in painting was closely tied to the wider intellectual currents of the Renaissance.

questions.q: How did medieval painters typically treat the size of figures?
options: They drew all figures exactly the same size.
They drew important people larger regardless of position.
They made distant figures systematically smaller.
They avoided including human figures entirely.
questions.explanation: The passage states that in medieval painting important people were drawn larger than others regardless of where they stood.

questions.q: What observation is linear perspective based on?
options: Colors appear brighter in the foreground of a scene.
Parallel lines appear to converge toward a vanishing point.
Shadows always fall toward the viewer.
Objects change color as they move closer.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that linear perspective is based on the observation that parallel lines appear to converge toward a single vanishing point and that distant objects appear smaller.

questions.q: Who is credited with demonstrating the principles of linear perspective?
options: Masaccio
Filippo Brunelleschi
An anonymous medieval monk
A group of Renaissance mathematicians
questions.explanation: The passage states that Filippo Brunelleschi is usually credited with demonstrating the principles of linear perspective, while Masaccio was among the first to apply them.

questions.q: According to the passage, what broader cultural interest did perspective reflect?
options: A renewed focus on spiritual meaning over realism
An interest in mathematics, observation, and rational order
A rejection of geometry in favor of intuition
A desire to return to medieval artistic styles
questions.explanation: The passage says perspective reflected a broader cultural interest in mathematics, observation, and the rational ordering of the world.

title: Why Coral Reefs Are Turning White
passage:
Coral reefs are among the most diverse ecosystems on Earth, supporting thousands of species of fish, invertebrates, and other marine life. Although a coral may look like a rock or a plant, it is actually a colony of tiny animals called polyps. Most reef-building corals share their tissues with microscopic algae known as zooxanthellae. Through photosynthesis, these algae produce sugars that provide the coral with much of its energy, and in return the coral offers the algae a sheltered place to live. This partnership also gives corals their vivid colors.

When ocean conditions become stressful, however, this relationship can break down. If the water grows too warm for even a few weeks, corals expel the algae living in their tissues. Without the colorful algae, the coral's white skeleton shows through, a phenomenon called coral bleaching. A bleached coral is not immediately dead, but it is weakened and deprived of its main source of food. If stressful conditions persist, the coral may starve and die; if conditions improve quickly enough, the algae can return and the coral may recover.

Rising ocean temperatures linked to climate change have made bleaching events more frequent and severe in recent decades. Large stretches of reef that once teemed with life have turned pale and, in some cases, died. Because reefs shelter coastlines from storms and support fisheries that millions of people depend on, their decline has consequences that extend well beyond the underwater world. Scientists are studying whether some corals can adapt to warmer water, but many warn that reducing greenhouse gas emissions is essential to protecting reefs over the long term.

questions.q: What is a coral, according to the passage?
options: A type of underwater plant
A colony of tiny animals called polyps
A kind of algae that produces sugar
A rock formation shaped by ocean currents
questions.explanation: The passage states that although a coral may look like a rock or plant, it is actually a colony of tiny animals called polyps.

questions.q: What causes coral bleaching?
options: Corals expel their algae when water becomes too warm.
Fish eat the colorful algae off the coral.
Storms scrape the color from the coral skeleton.
Corals stop producing their own pigment as they age.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that when water grows too warm, corals expel the algae in their tissues, and the white skeleton shows through, which is bleaching.

questions.q: According to the passage, is a bleached coral necessarily dead?
options: Yes, bleaching kills the coral instantly.
No, it is weakened but may recover if conditions improve.
Yes, because the algae can never return.
No, because bleaching does not affect the coral's food supply.
questions.explanation: The passage says a bleached coral is not immediately dead but weakened, and if conditions improve quickly the algae can return and the coral may recover.

questions.q: Why does reef decline matter beyond the underwater world?
options: Reefs are the main source of oxygen on Earth.
Reefs shelter coastlines and support fisheries people depend on.
Reefs prevent ocean temperatures from rising.
Reefs are the only home of zooxanthellae algae.
questions.explanation: The passage notes that reefs shelter coastlines from storms and support fisheries that millions of people depend on, so their decline has wider consequences.

title: The Bystander Effect
passage:
In social psychology, the bystander effect describes a surprising pattern: the more people who witness an emergency, the less likely any single person is to offer help. One might expect that a larger crowd would mean more potential helpers and therefore a faster response. Research, however, has repeatedly found the opposite. When many bystanders are present, each individual is less likely to intervene, and help may be delayed or never come at all.

Psychologists have proposed several explanations for this behavior. One is the diffusion of responsibility: when many people are present, each person feels less personally responsible for acting, assuming that someone else will step in. Another factor is social influence. People often look to those around them to decide how to behave in an ambiguous situation. If no one else appears alarmed, an individual may conclude that the situation is not actually an emergency, even when it is. A third factor is the fear of embarrassment, since people worry about overreacting or looking foolish in front of others.

These explanations were developed after researchers conducted controlled experiments in which participants faced staged emergencies, sometimes alone and sometimes in groups. Consistently, participants who believed they were alone helped more quickly and more often than those who thought others were present. Understanding the bystander effect has practical value: safety experts now advise that a person needing help should single out one specific individual and make a direct request, rather than appealing to a crowd in general. Naming one person cuts through the diffusion of responsibility and makes it far more likely that help will arrive.

questions.q: What does the bystander effect describe?
options: People help faster when more witnesses are present.
People are less likely to help when more witnesses are present.
People always help strangers in emergencies.
People help only when they are part of a large group.
questions.explanation: The passage defines the bystander effect as the pattern in which the more people who witness an emergency, the less likely any single person is to help.

questions.q: What is meant by 'diffusion of responsibility'?
options: People feel more responsible when others are watching.
Each person feels less responsible, assuming someone else will act.
Responsibility is shared equally and help comes quickly.
Only trained professionals feel responsible in emergencies.
questions.explanation: The passage explains diffusion of responsibility as each person feeling less personally responsible when many are present, assuming someone else will step in.

questions.q: How can social influence discourage helping in an ambiguous situation?
options: People imitate others who rush to help.
People conclude it is not an emergency if no one else seems alarmed.
People become more alarmed than the situation warrants.
People leave the scene to find authorities.
questions.explanation: The passage states that if no one else appears alarmed, an individual may conclude the situation is not an emergency, even when it is.

questions.q: What practical advice follows from research on the bystander effect?
options: Wait for a large crowd to gather before asking for help.
Single out one specific person and make a direct request.
Avoid asking for help in public places.
Appeal to the crowd in general as loudly as possible.
questions.explanation: The passage advises that a person needing help should single out one specific individual and make a direct request, which cuts through diffusion of responsibility.

title: Library Notice: Extended Exam Hours
passage:
ATTENTION ALL STUDENTS

Starting Monday, May 4, the Central Library will extend its hours to support students during the final exam period. From May 4 through May 22, the library will be open from 7:00 a.m. until 2:00 a.m., Monday through Friday. Weekend hours will be 9:00 a.m. to midnight.

Please note that the third-floor Quiet Study Zone will be reserved for silent individual study only. Group work and discussions should take place in the second-floor Collaboration Rooms, which can be booked online up to three days in advance.

Free coffee and tea will be available in the ground-floor lounge each night after 9:00 p.m. while supplies last. Remember to bring your student ID, as it is required for entry after 10:00 p.m.

We wish everyone success on their exams!

questions.q: What is the main purpose of this notice?
options: To announce the closing of the library for repairs
To inform students about extended hours during exams
To explain how to apply for a new student ID
To advertise a coffee shop near the library
questions.explanation: The notice announces extended library hours to support students during the final exam period, which is its main purpose.

questions.q: Where should students go to do group work?
options: The third-floor Quiet Study Zone
The ground-floor lounge
The second-floor Collaboration Rooms
The Central Library entrance
questions.explanation: The notice states that group work and discussions should take place in the second-floor Collaboration Rooms.

questions.q: What is required for entry after 10:00 p.m.?
options: A booking confirmation
A student ID
A cup of coffee
A library membership fee
questions.explanation: The notice says to bring your student ID, as it is required for entry after 10:00 p.m.

questions.q: When is free coffee and tea available?
options: All day in the ground-floor lounge
Each night after 9:00 p.m. while supplies last
Only on weekends at midnight
In the Collaboration Rooms after booking
questions.explanation: The notice states that free coffee and tea will be available in the ground-floor lounge each night after 9:00 p.m. while supplies last.

title: Email: Change to Group Project Meeting
passage:
From: Maria Alvarez
To: Project Team
Subject: Change to Thursday's Meeting

Hi everyone,

I wanted to let you know that our usual meeting room, Room 210, is unavailable this Thursday because the department is using it for a workshop. I've booked Room 305 instead, which is one floor up. The meeting time stays the same, 4:00 p.m.

Before we meet, please finish reading the two articles Dr. Chen shared last week so we can discuss them. Kevin, could you bring the printed survey results? We'll need them to plan the next section of the report.

Also, a quick reminder that our final presentation slides are due to Dr. Chen by next Monday, so let's aim to finalize them on Thursday.

See you all there,
Maria

questions.q: Why is the meeting room changing this Thursday?
options: Room 210 is being repaired.
The department is using Room 210 for a workshop.
The team wanted a larger room.
Dr. Chen requested a different location.
questions.explanation: The email states that Room 210 is unavailable because the department is using it for a workshop.

questions.q: What is Kevin asked to bring?
options: The two articles from Dr. Chen
The final presentation slides
The printed survey results
Coffee for the team
questions.explanation: Maria asks Kevin to bring the printed survey results, which the team needs to plan the next section of the report.

questions.q: What should team members do before the meeting?
options: Finish reading the two articles Dr. Chen shared
Reserve Room 305 themselves
Submit the final slides to Dr. Chen
Print copies of the report
questions.explanation: The email asks everyone to finish reading the two articles Dr. Chen shared so they can discuss them at the meeting.

questions.q: When are the final presentation slides due?
options: This Thursday at 4:00 p.m.
By next Monday
During the department workshop
At the end of the semester
questions.explanation: The email reminds the team that the final presentation slides are due to Dr. Chen by next Monday.

title: Campus Shuttle Schedule Update
passage:
CAMPUS SHUTTLE - SUMMER SCHEDULE

Beginning June 1, the campus shuttle will run on a reduced summer schedule. The shuttle will operate Monday through Friday only; there will be no weekend service until the fall term begins.

On weekdays, shuttles depart from the Main Gate every 30 minutes between 7:30 a.m. and 6:00 p.m. The route now includes a new stop at the Science Complex, added in response to student requests. Please note that the stop at the old Sports Center has been removed for the summer, since that building is closed for renovation.

Riders can track shuttle locations in real time using the CampusGo app, available for free download. If a shuttle is running more than ten minutes late, an alert will appear in the app. For questions, contact the Transportation Office at the Student Center.

questions.q: What is the main change described in this notice?
options: The shuttle will stop running permanently.
The shuttle will follow a reduced summer schedule.
The shuttle will only run on weekends.
The shuttle will become a paid service.
questions.explanation: The notice announces that beginning June 1 the campus shuttle will run on a reduced summer schedule.

questions.q: Which new stop has been added to the route?
options: The Sports Center
The Main Gate
The Science Complex
The Student Center
questions.explanation: The notice states that a new stop at the Science Complex was added in response to student requests.

questions.q: Why was the Sports Center stop removed?
options: The building is closed for renovation.
No students used that stop.
It was too far from the Main Gate.
The shuttle route became too long.
questions.explanation: The notice explains that the old Sports Center stop was removed because that building is closed for renovation.

questions.q: How can riders find out if a shuttle is running late?
options: By calling the Main Gate
By checking an alert in the CampusGo app
By visiting the Science Complex
By reading a printed schedule at the stop
questions.explanation: The notice says that if a shuttle is more than ten minutes late, an alert will appear in the CampusGo app.

title: Advertisement: Community Cooking Classes
passage:
LEARN TO COOK WITH RIVERSIDE COMMUNITY CENTER

Have you always wanted to cook delicious meals at home? Join our popular evening cooking classes this fall! Whether you are a complete beginner or looking to sharpen your skills, we have a class for you.

Beginner classes meet on Tuesday evenings from 6:00 to 8:00 p.m. and focus on simple, healthy weeknight dinners. Intermediate classes meet on Thursday evenings and explore dishes from around the world. Each session is led by a professional chef, and all ingredients and equipment are provided.

Classes cost $15 per session, or $120 for a full ten-week term, a saving of thirty dollars. Space is limited to twelve students per class to ensure everyone gets personal attention. To reserve your spot, sign up at the front desk or call us at 555-0199. Aprons are provided, but please wear closed-toe shoes for safety.

questions.q: Who are these cooking classes intended for?
options: Only professional chefs
Both beginners and those improving their skills
Only children under twelve
Only people who own restaurants
questions.explanation: The advertisement says the classes are for complete beginners or those looking to sharpen their skills, so both groups are welcome.

questions.q: What do the Thursday intermediate classes focus on?
options: Simple weeknight dinners
Dishes from around the world
Baking desserts only
Vegetarian cooking
questions.explanation: The advertisement states that intermediate classes meet on Thursday evenings and explore dishes from around the world.

questions.q: How much can a student save by paying for the full ten-week term?
options: Fifteen dollars
Thirty dollars
One hundred twenty dollars
Nothing; the prices are the same
questions.explanation: The full term costs $120 instead of $15 per session for ten weeks ($150), a saving of thirty dollars, as the ad states.

questions.q: What are students asked to wear for safety?
options: An apron they bring themselves
Closed-toe shoes
A chef's hat
Gloves and a mask
questions.explanation: The advertisement asks students to wear closed-toe shoes for safety, noting that aprons are already provided.

title: Notice: New Recycling Rules in the Dormitory
passage:
IMPORTANT: NEW RECYCLING RULES

Starting next week, our dormitory will follow new recycling guidelines set by the city. Please read carefully, as sorting waste correctly helps our building avoid fines.

There will now be three separate bins on each floor. The blue bin is for paper and cardboard, the green bin is for glass, metal, and plastic containers, and the gray bin is for general trash that cannot be recycled. Food waste should NOT go in any of the recycling bins; instead, please use the compost bin located in the kitchen on each floor.

Before placing containers in the green bin, rinse them so that leftover food does not attract insects. Flatten cardboard boxes to save space in the blue bin. If you are unsure where something belongs, a sorting guide is posted above the bins. Thank you for helping keep our building clean and reducing waste.

questions.q: Why are the new recycling rules being introduced?
options: To follow new city guidelines and avoid fines
To reduce the number of bins on each floor
To close the dormitory kitchens
To charge students a recycling fee
questions.explanation: The notice explains that the dormitory will follow new city guidelines and that sorting correctly helps the building avoid fines.

questions.q: Which items go in the green bin?
options: Paper and cardboard
Glass, metal, and plastic containers
Food waste
General trash that cannot be recycled
questions.explanation: The notice states that the green bin is for glass, metal, and plastic containers.

questions.q: What should students do with food waste?
options: Put it in the gray bin
Put it in the blue bin
Use the compost bin in the kitchen
Rinse it and put it in the green bin
questions.explanation: The notice says food waste should not go in any recycling bin and should instead go in the compost bin in the kitchen.

questions.q: Why should containers be rinsed before recycling?
options: So they can be reused by other students
So leftover food does not attract insects
Because the city charges for dirty containers
To make them easier to flatten
questions.explanation: The notice advises rinsing containers so that leftover food does not attract insects.
